text: My boss told me I did great work on the project but she says that to everyone so it doesn't really mean anything.
I got an A on the exam but it was an easy test, honestly anyone could have passed it.
He said he loves me but I think he's just saying it because that's what you're supposed to say.
I finished the half marathon but the course was flat and the weather was perfect. That's not a real accomplishment.
People keep telling me I'm doing well but they don't actually see what's going on inside.
She complimented my cooking but she's the kind of person who's nice about everything. She didn't mean it.
I won the award at work but I'm pretty sure it was just politics. The people who actually deserved it didn't get it.
My therapist says I've made real progress but I feel like I'm just getting better at pretending.
I got promoted but I think they just needed someone to fill the role and I happened to be there.
Yes I've been having better days lately but those don't count. Good days don't erase everything else.
My friends threw me a birthday party and said really nice things but I know they felt obligated. That's what you do for a friend.
I managed to go a whole week without a panic attack but one good week doesn't prove anything about my anxiety.
He said I was the best partner he's ever had but he's only dated a few people. That doesn't mean much.
I landed my first client for my freelance business but it was only because she's my cousin's friend. She felt sorry for me.
My painting sold at the gallery show but they were probably just trying to be supportive of local artists.
I finally spoke up in the team meeting and people responded positively, but they were just being nice because I'm new.
My therapist says what I went through was genuinely hard and I handled it well. But she has to say things like that.
My mom said she's proud of me but she's my mom. Of course she'd say that.
I completed the online course but it was self-paced and open to everyone. Getting a certificate doesn't prove I know anything.
People said the speech I gave at the wedding was really moving but I think they were just caught up in the emotion of the day.
I've been clean for six months which people keep congratulating me on but I've done it before and relapsed. It doesn't count until I know it will stick.
She told me I'm one of the kindest people she knows but she doesn't know me that well. If she did she'd see what I'm really like.
I got into the grad program but it's a new program with lower standards. Real PhD programs wouldn't have accepted me.
My coworkers said they really appreciate having me on the team but that's just office stuff. They don't actually mean it personally.
I managed to save some money this month for the first time in years, but it's such a small amount. It's not like it makes a difference.
My doctor said my test results look much better and I should feel good about the progress. I do feel physically better, but I don't trust it. It could reverse.
I helped my neighbor move and she said she doesn't know what she would have done without me, but I just helped load a truck. That's not a big deal.
My writing professor gave my story the highest grade in the class and read it aloud as an example, but the other students are not writers. It doesn't mean my writing is actually good.
I performed at the open mic and people clapped and some even came up to me afterward, but people clap for everyone. That's just what you do.
I've gotten pretty good feedback on my work lately but I think I just got lucky with the assignments. The next project will expose me.
My son told me I was the best dad he could have asked for. He's nine years old. He doesn't know any better.
I ran my first 5K last weekend and I came in faster than I expected, but I walked parts of it. Real runners don't walk.
My sister says I'm the strong one in the family, that everyone relies on me. But that's only because I hide how much I'm struggling. They'd think differently if they knew.
I got through the whole week at the new job without a breakdown, which everyone is calling a win. But I barely spoke to anyone and spent lunches hiding in my car.
He told me he's never felt this way about anyone. But he's young and hasn't been with many people. It's not a real comparison.
I got the job offer I'd been hoping for but I think they interviewed only a few candidates. It wasn't that competitive.
The psychiatrist said I should feel proud of how I've managed my medication this year. But managing medication is the bare minimum. I don't deserve praise for that.
I did well in the presentation and my manager said she was impressed, but I had three days to prepare. That's not a fair test of anything.
My support group keeps saying how much I've grown since I started coming. But I only look better in comparison to how bad I was before. I'm not actually doing well.
He says I make him happy every single day, but he's just an optimistic person. He'd probably say that about anyone he was with.
I got a raise this year but it was a cost-of-living adjustment. Everyone got one. That's not the same as being valued.
My friends say I give the best advice and they always come to me with their problems, but I can see what's wrong with everyone else's life. I just can't fix my own.
I cooked a full Thanksgiving dinner from scratch and everyone said it was amazing, but I used recipes and watched YouTube videos the whole time. I didn't do it from skill.
My recovery coach said I should be proud because a lot of people don't make it this far. But I don't feel like I've done anything. I've just been not dying.
I wrote 10,000 words on my novel this month which is the most I've ever written. But they're rough draft words. They're not real.
I've started working out regularly and my trainer said my strength has improved significantly. But I'm comparing myself to how out of shape I was. That's a low bar.
She said that talking to me helped her more than she expected. But I just listened. Anyone can listen. That's not a skill.
I won the chess tournament at the community center but these are hobbyists. If I played in a real tournament I'd lose immediately.
People at the party said they loved my energy and that I was so fun to be around. But I'd had two drinks. That wasn't really me.
My sister tells me I'm a great mom but she doesn't see the days when I completely lose it with the kids. She only sees the good parts.
I managed to go back to my childhood home for the first time since my parents divorced. My therapist said that took real courage. I don't see it that way. I just drove there.
I got through the hospital stay without completely falling apart. People keep saying how strong I was. I wasn't strong. I just had no other choice.
My ex reached out to say that I was actually the best relationship he'd had. But he's had terrible relationships so that's not saying much.
I gave a talk at the conference and got good ratings on the feedback forms, but conference attendees are too polite to give negative feedback.
I've been consistently showing up for my friends this year and they've all noticed and thanked me for it. But showing up is just what you're supposed to do. I shouldn't get credit for basic decency.
I got a scholarship but it's a small regional one. It's not like I got into a national program or anything.
My manager said I handled the difficult client better than anyone else on the team could have. But I think I just got lucky with what the client needed that day.
I asked someone out and they said yes, which my friends are treating like a huge victory. But it's just a date. It doesn't mean they actually like me.
I set a boundary with my family for the first time and stuck to it. My therapist was really pleased. But I only did it over text. I couldn't have done it in person.
I got positive comments on my portfolio but these are people who follow me on social media. They're already biased toward liking my work.
I got into the best shape of my life this year and people constantly compliment me on it, but I was at a really unhealthy starting point. I'm just less unhealthy now.
She said I'm one of the most emotionally intelligent people she's ever met. But she was probably just saying that because we were having a deep conversation and the moment called for it.
I passed my driver's test on the first try which my parents were thrilled about, but I'm twenty-six years old and this was long overdue. It's embarrassing, not impressive.
Everyone said the dinner I hosted was perfect. But I had a Pinterest board for a month beforehand. It's not like I'm naturally talented at hosting.
I haven't self-harmed in four months. My counselor said that's a major milestone. But I still think about it every day. Not doing it isn't the same as being okay.
My manager said the client specifically asked for me to lead the project. But maybe they just don't know anyone else on the team well enough yet.
He said he has never trusted anyone as quickly as he trusted me. I think it means he has poor judgment about people, not that I'm actually trustworthy.
I got through the job interview without freezing up which was a big deal for me, but I still didn't get the job. So it doesn't count.
My professor nominated me for an award for the paper I wrote. I think she nominates everyone who does decent work just to support the program.
I had a great first date and he texted right after saying how much fun he had, but first dates are easy. The real test comes when someone actually gets to know me.
I graduated with honors which my family made a huge deal of. I chose an easy major on purpose. If I'd done engineering like I should have, I would have failed.
I successfully advocated for myself at the doctor's office and got a referral I needed. My friend said I was amazing. But I'd prepared for weeks. Normal people can just do that without preparation.
My coworker said she wishes she had half my patience. But I'm only patient because I'm too anxious to speak up. It's not actually a virtue.
I finally cleaned the apartment top to bottom and my partner said the place looked amazing. But this is what it should look like all the time. Doing it once doesn't make me a functional person.
I've started to enjoy things again after years of depression. My doctor called it a breakthrough. But I know it can go away. So I don't let myself feel good about it.
My grandmother said I was her favorite grandchild, which I know I shouldn't take seriously because grandparents say things like that.
I mentored a junior colleague who said she learned more from me than from any training she'd done. But I just told her what I wished someone had told me. That's not expertise.
I played a small role in a community theater production and people told me I stole the show. But the leads were mediocre. Standing out wasn't hard.
I submitted my first photography project and the instructor gave it the best grade in the class. But she grades on effort and improvement, not actual quality.
I've been journaling consistently for three months which my therapist calls one of the most positive changes she's seen in me. But writing in a notebook is not an achievement.
She said my poem made her cry and that she wanted to share it with people. I think it was just hitting her at a vulnerable moment. It wasn't the poem.
I turned down a drink at a party for the first time. People in my recovery group congratulated me but it was one drink at one party. That's not sobriety.
My therapist said I've shown real courage in facing things I've been avoiding for years. But I only face them in a therapy session, not in real life. That doesn't count.
I stayed calm during a really stressful situation at work that would have sent me into a spiral before. My partner said she was proud of me. But I was just numb, not calm.

My academic advisor said my research proposal was among the strongest she'd seen from a student at my level. But I worked on it for months and had a lot of help. On my own I couldn't produce anything like it.
I opened up to a friend about something I've never told anyone, and she thanked me for trusting her. But she just made it easy. Any decent person would have.
I've been eating regularly for the past month which my dietitian says is a significant change. But eating three meals a day is the bare minimum. I'm not recovered just because I managed to do that.
I got a standing ovation after my recital. I was already planning what I'd done wrong before I even sat back down.
She said she feels safe with me and that I'm the most stable presence in her life. I just don't show her my bad days. It's not real.

I hit a personal best at the gym this week. My trainer was excited. But I've been lifting for two years. I should be much further along. A personal best now is just evidence of how slow my progress has been.
My doctor told me the scan results showed significant improvement and that I should feel very encouraged. I heard the good news and immediately thought about what could still be wrong.
I got a positive response to my cold email to a company I really want to work for. But they respond to everyone. That's just professional courtesy.
My therapist said the way I handled my father's visit was remarkably mature. But I was just shutting down. That's not maturity. That's dissociation.
I paid off my credit card which I've been trying to do for three years. My partner hugged me and called it huge. But it took me three years. It's not something to celebrate.
My friends say I always know what to say when they're upset. But I've been through so much that I just know the right words. It's not a strength. It's just damage.
I wrote a letter to my estranged parent. I didn't send it. My therapist said the act of writing it was meaningful. I don't see how something I didn't send means anything.
I had a good week. I keep waiting for something to ruin it. Good weeks don't mean anything because they always end.
I got recognized by the department for going above and beyond. But I stayed late and worked weekends for months to earn that. It should have been expected of me, not rewarded.

I told my parents how their behavior affected me growing up. My therapist called it one of the bravest things she'd heard a patient do. But I've been building up to it for two years. It's not brave if it takes that long.
I launched my small business and got my first real paying customer this week. But they're a friend who wanted to support me. That doesn't prove the business is viable.
I went to the gym four days this week which hasn't happened in years. My roommate said she was proud of me but she doesn't know how slowly I went or how little I actually did when I got there.
My instructor said my form was the best in the class. But the class is full of beginners. In a real class with serious students I'd be at the bottom.
I've learned to cook a few real meals and people enjoy eating them. But I follow recipes exactly. Take the recipe away and I have no idea what I'm doing.
My partner said that the way I handle my anxiety has actually helped him understand his own emotions better. I don't know how that's possible. My anxiety is a burden on both of us.
I gave blood for the first time. The nurse said a lot of people can't do it and I should be proud. But it's just donating blood. That's not an accomplishment.
I got cast in the play I auditioned for. But they were short on people. They needed bodies, not talent.
I'm told I'm a good listener. But I'm only quiet because I don't have anything valuable to add. It's not a skill.
I raised $2,000 for a charity I believe in. My friends said it was incredible. But I just shared a link. People donated. I didn't do anything.
My professor said my dissertation showed exceptional original thinking. But I had a very specific niche and there wasn't much prior work to compare to. That made it easier to seem original.
I got my GED at thirty-eight. My wife cried at the ceremony. But I should have done this twenty years ago. There's nothing to celebrate.
I ate lunch with my coworkers today instead of alone at my desk. My therapist said that was a real step forward. But I barely said anything the whole time.
I got into remission. My oncologist told me I'd fought hard and should feel proud. I keep thinking about recurrence rates. The remission doesn't feel real.
Everyone in my family says I hold us together but I think they mean I just don't cause problems. That's not the same as being strong.
I've been sleeping better for the past few weeks. My partner says I seem like a different person. But sleep problems always come back. I don't want to get used to this.
I fixed a bug in the codebase that three senior engineers had missed. My tech lead said it was impressive. But it was probably just luck that I spotted it.
I told someone I liked them and they said they felt the same way. But I had rehearsed it twenty times. If I'd had to do it spontaneously I would have failed.
My children told me they have the best mom in the world. They don't know any other moms very well. They have no basis for comparison.
I completed a detox program and my family was at the door to celebrate when I came out. But I've been here before. People being happy for me now doesn't change the past.
I got a perfect score on the test. My teacher said she'd never seen that before. But I'd studied the exact material that came up. If the questions had been different, I wouldn't have done well.
I'm one of the top performers on the sales team. But I have the easiest territory. The people with hard territories are the real achievers.
I apologized to someone I hurt a long time ago and they said it meant everything to them. But I took fifteen years to do it. That apology is too late to count as anything good.
I maintained my weight loss for a full year, which my doctor called a real success. But I'm still not where I want to be. Maintaining a loss that's not enough isn't an accomplishment.
He said I make him want to be a better person. I think that just means he sees how much he has to improve. It's not a compliment about me.

I finished reading a book this week, which sounds pathetic but for me with my attention issues it's actually hard. My therapist said it was great. But normal people do this all the time. It's not a real achievement.
I saved my colleague from making a really costly error. She thanked me and told our manager. But I just happened to look at her screen at the right time.
I went to my high school reunion and people said I looked great and seemed so happy. But I'd been preparing for it emotionally for a month. They were seeing a performance.
I've been making my bed every morning for two months. My therapist said small habits build into bigger changes. But making a bed is not a sign of health. Anyone can pull a blanket up.
I spoke to my estranged brother for the first time in four years. The call went well. But one good call doesn't fix anything. It doesn't count as reconciliation.
I designed a logo for a client and they said it was exactly what they'd imagined. I just got lucky interpreting their brief correctly. Next time I won't.

I got through my parents' visit without any major incidents. Everyone said how well I'd handled it. But I spent three days planning my responses to things they might say. That's not actually being okay.
My partner told me I'm the most caring person he's ever been with. But I care because I'm terrified of abandonment. It's not real love, it's anxiety.
I got a callback from the audition. My acting coach said I should be proud. But callbacks don't mean you got the part, and I probably won't.
I bench-pressed more than I thought I could this week. But I should be able to lift much more by now. A new personal record just shows how far behind I am.
I got through Christmas this year without the usual family meltdown. My therapist made a big deal of it. But I just avoided everyone more. That's not healthy.
People compliment me on being so resilient. But I don't have a choice. It's not resilience, it's just having no alternative.
I got a really heartfelt thank-you note from a patient's family after my shift. But I was just doing my job. That's what nurses are supposed to do.

I made it through the anniversary of the accident. My therapist called that a real milestone. But every year it gets slightly less terrible just from the passage of time. I didn't do anything to make that happen.
I published an article in a small journal. My colleagues congratulated me but it's a low-impact journal. Real researchers publish in top-tier outlets.
People say my garden looks beautiful. But I just follow the instructions on the seed packets. There's no skill in that.

I didn't freeze up during the performance anxiety attack I felt coming on. My therapist said that was a huge breakthrough. But I've had that attack a hundred times. At some point you just get used to hiding it.

I had a major depressive episode this year and still managed to keep my job. My family thinks that's remarkable. But I went to work and did the bare minimum. I wasn't thriving. I was surviving. Those are different things.
My coworker said I have a gift for explaining complicated things simply. But I just know the material well. Anyone would seem good at explaining if they knew it as well as I do.
I'm told I'm good at calming people down when they're in crisis. But I'm only calm around other people's crises because my own are so much bigger. It's not a skill. It's a survival mechanism.
I got a personal record in my swim time this month. But I've been doing this for years and this is still slow for someone with my training history.
My grandkids said they want to be just like me when they grow up. They don't know what I've done. If they did they'd feel differently.
I got a standing ovation at the end of my set. But the crowd was drunk. They would have clapped for anything.
I successfully navigated a panic attack in a public place without leaving. My therapist was thrilled. But I was white-knuckling it the whole time. That's not progress. That's just not running away.
I trained for six months and completed my first triathlon. My whole family came to watch. But I came in near the back. It doesn't mean much.
He told me he's never opened up to anyone the way he opens up to me. But some people are just like that with anyone who listens. It doesn't mean something special about me.
My team won the championship and they gave me the MVP award. But I scored the goals at the right time. If the timing had been different none of it would have mattered.
I passed the board exam on my first try. It was a hard exam. My parents cried when I called them. But I studied every day for months. Anyone who did that would have passed.
My friends say I inspire them but I'm just doing what I have to do to get through the day. There's nothing inspiring about necessity.
I made a major breakthrough in therapy today. My therapist said she was genuinely moved. But I know I'll probably go backward again next week. One session doesn't change who I am.

I've been consistently nice to myself for the last few weeks — catching negative self-talk and reframing it. My partner says he's noticed a difference. But I'm probably just suppressing it. It'll come out eventually.
I got through the IVF process without any major emotional breakdowns. My doctor praised my resilience. But I was numb the whole time. That's not resilience.
My editor said this is the best thing I've ever written. But I've written a lot of garbage so the bar isn't high.

I taught myself guitar over the past year and can play several full songs now. My family always wants me to play when we're together. But I can only play the easy versions of songs. Real guitarists would see right through that.

I've been staying off social media for three weeks after struggling with it for years. My therapist said that kind of discipline is remarkable. It's not discipline, I just deleted the apps. Anyone can delete an app.
My team lead said I have natural leadership instincts. But I was only leading because no one else wanted to. It's not a real compliment.
I finished chemo and my oncologist said I was one of the most positive patients he'd worked with. But I was faking it. I was terrified the whole time.
I got tenure. It should feel like a milestone. But I'm already thinking about all the ways I'm behind compared to my colleagues and all the publications I haven't written.
I got an offer from a company I really wanted to work at. But the recruiter said they had trouble filling the role. They probably had no other good options.
My sponsor said I've become an inspiration to newer members of the group. But I've just been around longer. Longevity isn't the same as having figured anything out.
I decorated my apartment and my friends said it looked amazing and asked if I'd done interior design. But I just followed things I'd pinned years ago. It's not creativity.
Everyone says I'm an amazing friend but I'm only good at showing up for others because it distracts me from my own problems. It's not actually selfless.
I got sober two years ago. My family says it's the greatest thing I've ever done. But I've put them through so much. Two years doesn't come close to making up for it.
She said meeting me changed her life. But she would have found her way eventually. I was just in the right place at the right time.
I got through my first panic attack in years without medication. My therapist seemed really pleased. But I know another one is coming. Getting through one doesn't mean I'm better.

I finally left a job that was making me miserable and found something better. Everyone said they were proud of me for making the change. But it took me four years to do something obvious. That's not admirable.
My professor said he wished more students had my work ethic. But I work hard because I'm terrified of failing. Fear isn't work ethic.

I stayed consistent with my medication for a whole year. My psychiatrist said that in itself was a real treatment success. But it's just taking pills. My eight-year-old does that without being praised.
I got a good review at work but I had an easy year. Wait until the real pressure comes.
My therapist said I've developed real emotional regulation skills. But I still struggle all the time. The skills only work sometimes. That's not really having them.
I ran the fastest mile of my life. My coach was ecstatic. But running faster than you used to isn't impressive. Other people my age run that fast without training.
People come to me for advice about relationships and say I always have the right answer. I just repeat things my therapist has told me. I'm not wise. I'm just a middleman.
I set a boundary with a toxic friend and held it for two months now. My therapist said growth like that is rare. But I only held it because I've been avoiding her, not because I've actually healed.
My boss said the way I handled the difficult client showed real maturity. But I was just too tired to fight. That's not maturity.

I haven't had a drink in ninety days. People in my life are making a big deal of it. But I don't feel like a different person. I'm just a person who hasn't had a drink in ninety days. Those are different things.
My students gave me a card at the end of the year saying I was the teacher who believed in them most. But I believed in them because they worked hard. I can't take credit for their hard work.
I bought my first home. My parents flew in for the closing. Everyone treated it like a huge deal. But I'm thirty-four. I should have done this years ago. It's embarrassing it took this long.

I got a positive reception when I shared my mental health story publicly. A lot of people reached out saying it resonated. But I think they responded to their own experiences, not to anything I specifically said.
I got the promotion I'd been working toward for three years. But I know there were better candidates. Someone must have advocated for me behind the scenes.
I finished my first ever marathon. I cried at the finish line. But I was dead last in my age group. Being a finisher when you finish last doesn't mean anything.
My kids always say my hugs fix everything. But they're kids. They think a lot of things that aren't true.
Everyone said the song I wrote was beautiful. But I wrote it about something really painful. The pain wrote the song. I didn't.
My friend said I've been her anchor this year. But she says that to make me feel good. She doesn't actually need me.
I won a community service award. But I did the work because I felt guilty, not because I'm generous. They're rewarding the wrong thing.
I've gotten into a routine of waking up at a consistent time. My therapist says that's a genuine sign of recovery. But a routine is what functional people just have. It's not recovery.
I caught myself before saying something I would have regretted in an argument with my partner. He noticed and appreciated it. But I shouldn't get credit for not doing something bad.
I solved a problem at work that had been stuck for weeks. My manager told the whole team. But I had a flash of insight. It wasn't skill. I can't repeat it on demand.
I just got the best feedback I've ever received on a project and my first thought was that the client must not have high standards.
My partner says she's luckier to have me than I am to have her. She's wrong. She just doesn't know it yet.
I got a full ride scholarship to the grad program I wanted. But it's a smaller school. If I'd applied to better schools I wouldn't have gotten in.
My therapist said she genuinely looks forward to our sessions. She's just saying that to build rapport. It's a technique.
I managed to keep all my friendships intact through a messy divorce. Everyone says that's impressive. But I just tried not to make it their problem. That's just keeping it together in public.

I haven't missed a day of work in six months despite everything going on. My manager noticed and mentioned it at the all-hands meeting. But showing up and functioning are not the same thing. I show up.
I got a commission for a mural. The client said they'd been looking for exactly what I do. But they probably hadn't looked very hard.
He said he's never met anyone like me. I know he means it as a compliment but I hear it as confirmation that I'm strange and not like normal people.

I finished the manuscript I've been working on for five years. My writing group said it was ready to send out. But I've been working on it for five years. That's not dedication. That's just taking too long.
I meditated every day this month for the first time ever. My therapist said it would compound over time. But I know myself. I'll stop eventually and it won't matter.
My coach said I've improved faster than any player she's coached. But I started with no background. Anyone who starts from zero improves fast at first. That's not talent.
People say I'm handling grief well. But I've cried every night. They're just not seeing it. I don't know why they think I'm doing well.
I reconnected with an old friend who said she'd missed me and wished we'd stayed closer. But I was the one who pulled away. She's being generous in not mentioning that.
I quit smoking after twenty years. My doctor said it's one of the best things a person can do for their health. But the damage is already done. It's too late for it to matter much.
My mentor said I'm ready to work independently and don't need her oversight anymore. I think she just wants to reduce her workload.
I finished therapy after three years. My therapist said she was genuinely proud of the work I did. But I still have bad days. If I'd really done the work, I wouldn't still struggle.
I got the highest grade in the class. The professor offered me a research assistant position. But this professor offers positions to anyone with good grades. It's not actually about me.
My partner said I'm his favorite person in the world. I smiled and said thank you but I was thinking he doesn't know me as well as he thinks he does.

I finally confronted my fear of heights by going on a hike I'd been avoiding for years. I made it to the top. My friends cheered. But I was terrified the entire time and swore I'd never do it again. That's not conquering something.
I was able to be present and enjoy my daughter's graduation without spiraling into anxiety. My therapist called it remarkable. But I'd taken an extra dose of my medication. That's not me doing well.
I've been told I'm one of the most empathetic people people have met. But I'm only empathetic because I've been through a lot of pain. It's not a virtue. It's just trauma.
I passed the bar on the first attempt. My family had a party for me. But I went to a school known for good bar passage rates. The school deserves the credit.
My partner said the way I take care of our home makes it feel like a real sanctuary. But I just keep things clean because mess triggers my anxiety. I'm not doing it for her.
I got off the waitlist and into the program I'd wanted. But someone else must have dropped out. I didn't earn the spot. I just benefited from someone else's decision.
I started going to therapy after resisting for years, and everyone says it took real courage to start. But I hit rock bottom first. It wasn't courage. I had no other options.
I've been managing my finances better than I ever have. My partner says she feels so much more secure. But I'm just barely keeping up. One emergency would wipe everything out.

I scored well on the standardized test. My tutor said she'd never had a student improve as much as I did. But I came from a very low baseline. Large improvements from a low starting point don't mean much.

I baked a really elaborate cake for my friend's birthday. She said it was the nicest thing anyone had ever done for her. But she doesn't have many people in her life right now. I'm not competing with much.
I've been keeping a food journal and my dietitian says it's making a huge difference. But I only started because I was forced to. If it was optional I would have quit.
I volunteered at a crisis hotline and a supervisor said I handled a difficult call better than most trained volunteers. But I just said things that felt obvious to me. I don't have special skills.
My partner said I've made real effort to change the patterns that were hurting our relationship. I have made effort. But effort without results doesn't count for much.
I got the internship at the company I really wanted. But my uncle knows someone there. Without that connection I wouldn't have even gotten an interview.
I got a compliment from someone I really respect today. All I could think was that she doesn't know the full picture. If she did, she'd think differently of me.
I've been described as someone who lights up a room. But that's only in certain situations. In others I'm invisible. The version of me they're complimenting isn't the real one.
I managed to host the family holiday dinner this year and everyone had a wonderful time. But it worked because the family was on good behavior this year. I didn't make that happen.
I've been doing well at my new job and my probationary review was glowing. But probationary periods are designed to be easy. The real work starts after that.
I got my driving anxiety under control enough to drive on the highway by myself. My therapist called it a major milestone. But I go at quiet times and on familiar routes. That's cheating.
I got a scholarship that covers all my tuition. My parents are over the moon. But I applied to so many scholarships. Eventually you have to get one if you apply to enough.
I wrote a post about my experience with mental health and it got shared thousands of times. But it went viral because of timing. Something else was trending and it got swept up in that.
I got my anxiety down enough to attend a concert for the first time in years. My best friend cried when I texted her. But I was miserable the whole time and left at the first break.
My boss said I'm the employee she trusts most. But she's said that to people before and they've disappointed her. I'm still in a phase where she doesn't know me well enough.

I was able to have a difficult conversation with my mother that went surprisingly well. My therapist was really pleased. But my mother was in a good mood that day. It was just luck. We'll have the same conversation again and it won't go well.
People tell me I'm very self-aware. But being self-aware about your problems and actually fixing them are completely different things. I can see all my flaws clearly and still can't change them.

I've been getting up every morning at 6am for a month. That used to be impossible for me. My therapist says it's a sign of real progress. But it's just an alarm. I'm not better, I'm just not sleeping in anymore.
My best friend said she doesn't know who she'd be without me. She's always been fine. She'd figure it out. I'm not irreplaceable.
I got a positive response to my grant application. But these panels approve a lot of proposals at this stage. The real rejection comes in the full review.
I went back to the place where something bad happened to me and I didn't break down. My therapist treated it like a breakthrough. But I just felt nothing. Numbness isn't the same as healing.
He said I had the most beautiful eyes he'd ever seen. I can't just accept that. I kept thinking he probably says things like that all the time.
I got through my first full year of sobriety. My family made a huge deal of it. But one year is just one year. People relapse after five. One year doesn't mean anything yet.

I stayed composed during an extremely stressful board presentation. Colleagues came up after and said they couldn't believe how calm I seemed. They'd be shocked to know I threw up in the bathroom beforehand. That wasn't composure.
My professor wrote me a glowing recommendation letter. But professors write those letters to support their department, not because they genuinely believe in the student.
I've been told I'm wise beyond my years. But I've only had experiences that forced me to grow up early. There's nothing admirable about being forced to mature.
I had a week where I genuinely felt okay. Not pretending, just actually okay. I'm suspicious of it. Something that good has to mean something bad is coming.

I got a really kind message from a reader who said my blog changed how they think about their own mental health. I probably just said something they were already thinking. I can't take credit for their insight.
My sponsor said I should be proud of how far I've come. But I don't feel proud. Pride feels like it erases the damage I did when I was using. I don't deserve to feel good about this.

I've started to have more good days than bad days for the first time in years. My partner says it's amazing to see. But good days don't cancel out the bad ones that came before. They don't mean I'm okay.
I got my work published in an anthology. My name is in print. But it's a self-published anthology with low standards. Being in it doesn't mean my work is good.
I completed a full year without a hospitalization, which my psychiatrist said was the longest stretch in my record. But I was probably just better at hiding it this year.

She told me she fell in love with me because of who I am, not what I look like or what I have. I wanted to believe her. But people say that and then leave when the reality of being with someone like me sets in.
I've been told by multiple therapists now that I have a lot of insight into myself. But insight without change is just knowing in detail all the ways you're broken. It's not an asset.
I got a raise that I hadn't expected. My manager said she'd been advocating for it for months because she thought I deserved it. But managers do that to retain employees. It's not personal.

I competed in my first jiu-jitsu tournament and won my division. My coach said he'd never seen a newer student do that well. But I'm older and heavier than most beginners so I had a physical advantage. It wasn't skill.
I've been consistently present for my kids this year in a way I wasn't last year. They've noticed and said things that hit me right in the heart. But one good year doesn't undo the years before.

He noticed I seemed off and asked what was wrong and actually listened. It was the nicest thing anyone had done in a long time. But he was probably just being polite and didn't know how to end the conversation.
I tried the new thing my therapist suggested and it actually helped a little. But it'll only work until I get used to it. All the techniques stop working eventually for me.

I gave a presentation on something I care deeply about and afterward several people said they'd learned a lot. But I'm passionate about it so of course I can talk about it. That doesn't make me good at presenting.
I got a standing ovation when I graduated. So did everyone else.
I've been trying to practice gratitude and some days it actually makes a difference. My therapist seems hopeful. But I can't sustain it. The good days are the exception, not the rule.
My professor said I ask the best questions in class. But I overthink everything and my questions come from anxiety, not curiosity. He's mistaking my neurosis for intelligence.
I feel a little better today but I've felt better before and it didn't mean anything. I don't want to get my hopes up.
People say I'm strong for going through all of this and still showing up. But strong is a word people use when they don't know what else to say. It doesn't help anything.
I honestly had a good day today for the first time in months. And I immediately started thinking about what I must be ignoring for things to feel this okay.
My boss told me she specifically hired me because of my skills. But she couldn't find anyone else who would take the salary they were offering. I was available. That was my qualification.
I ran the whole 10K without stopping, which I've never done before. The time wasn't great, but I ran every step. My partner and I celebrated after. But I told myself it was too slow to really count.
I didn't get angry when my father said something hurtful at dinner last night. My therapist said that was huge progress. But I've just gotten better at shutting down. I'm not actually less hurt.
My coworker said working with me has restored her faith in the profession. That's too much to put on me. She's had a few bad colleagues. That sets a low bar.

I managed to host Thanksgiving sober for the first time. My whole family noticed and said they were proud of me. It was the hardest day of the year and I got through it without drinking. But I took a Xanax. That doesn't count as sober.

I turned down junk food I really wanted because I knew it would make me feel worse. Small choices matter according to my nutritionist. But one choice doesn't make me someone who takes care of themselves.
My friend group said I've really blossomed this year. But I've just gotten better at performing happiness. If they saw my apartment on a bad day they'd think differently.

I finished a big project on time for the first time in my career. My director sent a company-wide congratulations. But I worked through two weekends to do it. That should just have been expected, not celebrated.
I've been doing really well with the new routine my therapist recommended. She said she's genuinely pleased with my progress. All I can think is that she hasn't seen me at 3am.
I made it through the hardest year of my life. People keep saying how proud they are of me. But surviving isn't an accomplishment. It's what happens when you don't have a way out.
People at work call me the glue that holds the team together. But they'd hold together fine without me. I'm just doing what anyone in my role would do.
I've been getting compliments on my parenting and honestly it makes me uncomfortable. I just do what my child needs. That's not special. That's just being a parent.
I feel better now than I did six months ago but I keep waiting for it to fall apart. Getting better never lasts for me.
I got through the hardest session in therapy I've ever had and didn't leave the room. My therapist said she was moved by my courage. But I was too frozen to leave. That's not the same thing.
He said I'm beautiful. I thanked him and then spent the next hour trying to figure out what he wants from me.

I got the highest performance rating in my review cycle. My manager said it reflected months of sustained excellence. But I had an unusually easy set of projects this cycle. It doesn't reflect what I'd do under real pressure.
My therapist said she thinks I'm finally ready to reduce our session frequency. I should take that as a good sign. I immediately wondered if I was being abandoned.
I got an A in organic chemistry which most people consider the hardest pre-med course. My parents called every relative. But the professor curved heavily. Without the curve I would have gotten a C.
I came in second in the competition. People said that was impressive for my first time competing. But first place is the only real result. Second place is just first loser.
My partner said I've changed and grown in ways she wasn't sure were possible a year ago. I have changed. But change isn't the same as being fixed. I'm still the same person underneath.

I got promoted to senior level. My salary went up significantly. My family was ecstatic. But the bar for promotion at my company is extremely low. They promote people to retain them, not because they're exceptional.
I've been told I'm one of the most dependable people in people's lives. But I'm only dependable because I'm afraid of letting anyone down. Fear isn't a virtue.

I had a breakthrough where something finally clicked in therapy. My therapist said she'd been waiting for that moment for months. But I'm scared to feel too good about it. I've had insights before that went nowhere.
People say I'm generous with my time and energy. But I give because I don't value my own time. That's not generosity. That's poor boundaries.
I tried something new and it actually went well. My first thought was that it must have been beginner's luck and it won't happen again.
My mentor said I'm ready to take on my own clients. I don't think I'm ready. She only thinks I am because she hasn't seen me work without her there.
I did well on the midterm. That's honestly the most suspicious thing that could happen. I never do well on midterms. Something must have gone wrong with the grading.

I said something comforting to a friend when she was really low, and she said it was exactly what she needed to hear. But the right words came out of nowhere. I don't even know where they came from. I can't claim credit for something I didn't consciously do.
Today was actually a good day and I don't know what to do with that. I feel like if I acknowledge it, I'll jinx it. Good things are always followed by something worse.
I had a week where I felt genuinely good but all I could think was that I must be ignoring something, filtering out the bad stuff. Good feelings have never been trustworthy for me.
I got a promotion and good feedback but I've been so focused on the one area where my boss said there's still room to grow that I can't actually feel happy about the rest of it.
I know people have said good things about my work but I can only remember the one negative comment from three months ago. I keep coming back to that and dismissing everything else.

I had a really successful week at work and plenty of people said so but I latched onto one moment where I stumbled in a meeting. That's the only part I can hold onto. The rest must not have been real.
I've been doing well by any external measure lately but all I can see are the things that are still wrong. The good things feel fake or accidental. The bad things feel like the truth.

People said my performance was strong overall but one reviewer gave me lukewarm feedback. I've been unable to stop thinking about that one reviewer. Everything else was probably just people being polite and she was the only one being honest.

I can see objectively that things have gotten better in several areas of my life. But I keep filtering for the areas that haven't improved and treating those as the real picture. Good outcomes don't feel like evidence of anything.

I have multiple people in my life who say positive things about me. I dismiss all of it automatically. If a single person says something negative, that feels accurate to me. I can't hold onto good things the way bad things hold onto me.
Objectively my relationship is going well and my partner is happy. I'm happy too, most of the time. But I keep looking for the cracks because I know it can't actually be this good.

I've been tracking my mood and the data shows clearly that I'm having more good days than bad. My therapist showed me the chart. But the bad days are the real ones. The good days are flukes. The chart doesn't change how I feel about it.

Something went well and I can't let myself feel good about it. I feel like acknowledging it will make me complacent and then something worse will happen to balance it out. I've never been able to just accept something good.

I've accomplished more this year than any previous year. My therapist laid it all out for me in a list. Looking at the list I just thought about how little it actually means given where I started from, and how behind I still am.
I know I've had some wins lately but whenever something goes right I can't really absorb it. My brain treats good news like background noise and amplifies everything that's still broken.

I've done well on tests all semester and people around me see it. But I keep returning to the one assignment I did poorly on at the start of the semester. That one is probably more representative of what I'm actually capable of.

I've had a lot of good news recently and I genuinely can't feel it. The good news just slides off me. Bad news sticks. I know that's not a fair way to evaluate my life but I can't seem to stop doing it.

I've been getting good feedback consistently for months and I dismiss every piece of it the same way. The failures feel like the truth about me. The successes feel like a temporary situation that will correct itself.
